title: NSA director: 'Mythos "broke into almost all of our classified systems in hours"
blurb: The NSA director said an AI system called Mythos broke into almost all of the agency's classified systems within hours, a claim surfacing as the Trump administration moves to block Anthropic. The Economist argues the decision is capricious and chaotic, leaving the government's posture toward frontier AI labs unsettled at a moment of acute security stakes.

title: New Exploit Bypasses Apple’s Boot Defenses, Affects Millions of iPhones
blurb: Researchers at Paradigm Shift disclosed Usbliter8, an unpatchable BootROM exploit that chains a USB controller bug and a firmware weakness to gain full code execution on iPhones with A12 and A13 chips, including the XS, XR, and 11. The attack requires physical USB access and cannot reach user data directly, but it bypasses Apple's secure boot chain and echoes 2019's Checkm8, making it valuable to forensics vendors.

title: Deno Desktop
blurb: Deno desktop, shipping in canary v2.9.0, turns any Deno project from a single TypeScript file to a Next.js app into a self-contained desktop binary that bundles your code, the runtime, and a web rendering engine. It auto-detects frameworks like Next.js, Astro, and SvelteKit, defaults to the OS webview for small binaries, uses in-process bindings instead of IPC, and ships built-in binary-diff auto-updates.

title: PixelSmash – Critical FFmpeg Vulnerability Turns Media Files into Weapons
blurb: JFrog Security Research disclosed PixelSmash (CVE-2026-8461), a critical heap out-of-bounds write in FFmpeg's MagicYUV decoder that achieves remote code execution from a single 50KB AVI file. Because FFmpeg's libavcodec is embedded in hundreds of downstream projects, the researchers confirmed crashes or full RCE against Jellyfin, Nextcloud, mpv, Kodi, Immich, and OBS, and recommend upgrading immediately or disabling the MagicYUV decoder at build time.

title: Klue hack results in data breach at several cybersecurity firms
blurb: Hackers breached Vancouver-based market intelligence provider Klue and stole data from an unspecified number of its corporate customers, including Gong, Jamf, HackerOne, OneTrust, Recorded Future, Snyk, and Tanium. The cybercrime group Icarus claimed the attack via a compromised legacy credential tied to a cloud integration tool, the latest in a wave of hacks targeting middleware vendors like Salesloft and Gainsight to reach hundreds of companies at once.

title: Inside NVIDIA Halos for Robotics: A Full-Stack Functional Safety System for Physical AI
blurb: NVIDIA announced Halos for Robotics, a full-stack functional safety platform built on the NVIDIA IGX Thor hardware and Halos OS, extending its autonomous-vehicle safety stack to humanoids, industrial robots, and autonomous mobile robots. Agility Robotics is integrating IGX Thor and Halos OS into the safe human-detection system on its Digit humanoid, signaling an industry shift from ad hoc safety toward a shared, standards-aligned foundation.

title: Skip Recompiling 70+ iOS Packages on Every Build
blurb: Expo SDK 56 ships Expo modules as precompiled XCFrameworks, cutting clean iOS build times with no migration needed.

title: Bank of England drops proposed holding caps for sterling stablecoins, sets £40 billion issuance guardrail
blurb: The Bank of England scrapped individual holding caps for sterling stablecoins, replacing them with a £40B systemic issuance guardrail ahead of a 2027 launch.

title: Canada’s Spy Agency Used First-of-Its-Kind Warrant to Clean Botnet-Infected Devices
blurb: Canada's spy agency, CSIS, used a first-of-its-kind threat reduction warrant to reach into Canadian-based servers, SOHO routers, and IoT devices like Ring doorbells and cameras and neutralize two foreign-run botnets, according to a Federal Court ruling made public on June 15. The court found the threat clearly established and proportional, and limited the operation to wiping botnet data from devices rather than targeting users or intercepting content.

title: Sakana Fugu
blurb: Sakana's Fugu experiment had an AI agent autonomously rewrite a small GPT's training recipe, running 123 experiments over roughly 14 hours on a single H100 and keeping only edits that lowered validation bits-per-byte. The Fugu-Ultra configuration, which orchestrates multiple frontier models, reached the best mean BPB of 0.9774 and a best single run of 0.9748, edging out every individual frontier-model baseline on agentic ML research.

title: Decades-Old Squid Proxy Flaw ‘Squidbleed’ Can Expose User Data
blurb: A Heartbleed-style memory leak in Squid Proxy, dubbed Squidbleed, can expose other users' cleartext HTTP credentials; patched in version 7.6.

title: OKX and NYSE partner to bridge tradfi and crypto markets in joint venture led by Andrew Cuomo
blurb: OKX and Intercontinental Exchange formed a joint venture, led by former New York Governor Andrew Cuomo, to build infrastructure connecting traditional and digital markets. Pending regulatory approval, the venture would operate as a US-registered broker-dealer and futures commission merchant, giving OKX's 120 million users access to ICE futures and NYSE tokenized equities following ICE's strategic investment in OKX at a $25B valuation.

title: Codex logging bug may write TBs to local SSDs
blurb: A GitHub issue reports that OpenAI's Codex continuously writes its SQLite feedback logs to disk, with one user measuring 37 TB written over 21 days of uptime, extrapolating to roughly 640 TB per year. TRACE-level logging accounts for about 70% of the retained bytes, and on a 1 TB consumer SSD rated near 600 TBW that volume could exhaust the drive's warranted write endurance in under a year.

title: Nvidia unveils Halos, a safety-focused OS developed from autonomous vehicle tech and designed to run on IGX Thor hardware for humanoid robots, and opens a lab (Ian King/Bloomberg)
blurb: Nvidia introduced Halos, a safety-focused operating system for humanoid robots adapted from its autonomous-vehicle technology and built to run on its IGX Thor hardware. The company also opened a dedicated safety lab, extending the self-driving stack it already sells to carmakers into the humanoid robotics market as it positions for that platform's next wave of demand.

title: MoneyGram joins Solana as validator amid stablecoin payment push
blurb: MoneyGram has become a validator on Solana, processing and securing transactions as it expands its stablecoin payments strategy. The move follows the recent launch of its MGUSD stablecoin on Stellar and a validator role on Tempo, signaling a shift from integrating crypto into remittances toward operating the networks that underpin those services.

title: Bank of England backs down on strict stablecoin holding limits, sets $50 billion issuance cap
blurb: The Bank of England dropped its proposed retail stablecoin holding limits, replacing them with a £40 billion ($50.6B) issuance cap per systemic coin.

title: Taiko halts its Ethereum layer-2 network after a bridge exploit, token dives
blurb: Taiko halted block production on its Ethereum layer-2 network and urged users to withdraw funds after an attacker forged cross-chain proofs to drain about $1.7M from its bridge. Security firm BlockSec traced the likely cause to a Raiko signing key left publicly accessible on GitHub, the same class of cross-chain messaging flaw behind more than $340M in bridge hacks this year.

title: Encrypted DNS still tells an eavesdropper where to look
blurb: Encrypted DNS hides query contents but plaintext packet headers still let an eavesdropper flag DNS flows; researchers propose header elision to close the gap.

title: Samsung Electronics brings ChatGPT and Codex to employees
blurb: Samsung Electronics is deploying ChatGPT Enterprise and Codex to employees worldwide, one of OpenAI's largest enterprise rollouts to date. The deal puts OpenAI's coding agent and chat assistant in front of a global workforce across Samsung's hardware and software divisions, deepening OpenAI's push into large-scale corporate contracts.

title: Getty Images Soars 200% in Early Trading After OpenAI Deal
blurb: Getty Images shares jumped roughly 200% in premarket trading Monday after the company announced a content-licensing deal with OpenAI. The agreement opens Getty's photography catalog to OpenAI's models, adding to a growing list of publishers and rights holders striking paid licensing arrangements rather than fighting AI training in court.

title: Apple Price Increases, Apple Intelligence and the E.U.
blurb: Apple is raising prices across its hardware lineup while declining to ship Apple Intelligence's Siri features to the E.U., citing regulatory constraints under the Digital Markets Act. The author argues the two moves are linked, with Apple using the E.U.'s rules as justification to withhold AI features even as it asks customers everywhere to pay more.

title: Meta Taps New WhatsApp Boss as Part of $900 Million Investment
blurb: Meta is investing $900M in Indian fintech startup Cred and plans to install founder Kunal Shah as the new head of WhatsApp. The move ties Meta's messaging platform more tightly to India, WhatsApp's largest market, and signals a renewed push to build payments and commerce features around the app.

title: Import AI 462: Superpersuasion; self-sustaining AI; paths to ASI
blurb: A new study from Oxford, AISI, Stanford, and LSE finds AI systems reliably out-persuade expert humans, including 3x professional charity canvassers.

title: Getty Images signs a licensing deal with OpenAI, letting its image library appear in ChatGPT's search and discovery tools; GETY jumps 90%+ and is down 10%+ YTD (Mark Bergen/Bloomberg)
blurb: Getty Images signed a licensing deal with OpenAI to bring its image library into ChatGPT's search and discovery tools. GETY jumped more than 90% on the news, though the stock remains down over 10% year-to-date, and the agreement gives OpenAI licensed visual content as rivals face mounting copyright litigation over training data.

title: Getty Images soars after striking a deal with the kind of company it sued
blurb: Getty Images shares jumped about 200% in premarket trading after it announced a multi-year deal to surface its licensed photos inside ChatGPT's search and discovery features. The agreement discloses no financial terms and pointedly says nothing about model training, the exact activity Getty spent years suing Stability AI over, marking a sharp pivot from courtroom opponent to OpenAI content partner.

title: Meta invests $900M into Indian fintech Cred for a ~20% stake, and plans to appoint Cred founder Kunal Shah as the leader of WhatsApp, replacing Will Cathcart (Kurt Wagner/Bloomberg)
blurb: Meta invested $900M into Indian fintech Cred for a roughly 20% stake and plans to install Cred founder Kunal Shah as the head of WhatsApp, replacing Will Cathcart. The move ties Meta's payments ambitions to one of India's largest consumer-credit platforms in WhatsApp's biggest market, where the company has long sought to scale commerce and payments.

title: Cuomo to Lead Crypto Venture With New York Stock Exchange Owner
blurb: Andrew Cuomo will help lead a new crypto venture jointly backed by exchange operator OKX and Intercontinental Exchange, the owner of the New York Stock Exchange. The business aims to merge digital-asset and traditional financial markets, marking a post-government pivot for the former New York governor into the intersection of Wall Street and crypto infrastructure.

title: Tesco races to migrate 40,000 servers away from VMware amid licensing spat
blurb: Tesco is migrating 40,000 servers off VMware "at exceptional pace" by end of 2027 amid a $130M licensing lawsuit against Broadcom.

title: Microsoft tells Windows users to get ready for 26H2 – unless they're on 26H1
blurb: Microsoft is telling Windows users to prepare for the 26H2 update, unless they're already running 26H1.

title: Seedcamp raises $320M for its new fund to expand its US footprint
blurb: Seedcamp raised $320M for Fund VII, its largest fund yet at double the $180M it raised in 2023, and will use it to expand its US presence. The European early-stage backer of Revolut, Wise, and UiPath is splitting the fund into $220M for first checks of around $1M and a new $100M growth vehicle for Series B and later follow-ons.

title: In a rare joint statement, Five Eyes leaders warn AI models capable of taking down governments and businesses are mere months away, urging leaders to "act now" (Sarah Basford Canales/The Guardian)
blurb: Five Eyes leaders issued a rare joint statement warning that AI models capable of crippling governments and businesses are only months away, urging policymakers to act now. The intelligence alliance, spanning the US, UK, Canada, Australia, and New Zealand, framed the threat as imminent rather than speculative, pressing for regulatory and security measures before such capabilities arrive.

title: John Ternus set to re-establish importance of Apple’s design team when he takes over as CEO: report
blurb: Incoming Apple CEO John Ternus plans to restore the design team's authority when he succeeds Tim Cook on September 1, according to Bloomberg's Mark Gurman. The move would reverse a decade in which operations and finance gained influence over product direction after Jony Ive's exit, and Ternus will debut as the public face of design with Apple's foldable iPhone this fall.

title: Copilot Cowork is now generally available: Everything you need to know, including pricing, usage limits, and new features
blurb: Microsoft made Copilot Cowork, its integration of Anthropic's Claude Cowork, generally available with model choice, plugins, and cost controls.

title: Sector Snapshot: Robotics Startups On Fire As Venture Funding Surges To Record Numbers In 2026
blurb: Robotics startups have raised $18.8B globally in 2026 so far, already topping the full-year records of both 2025 ($15B) and the 2021 peak ($14.1B) with six months still left. Investor appetite has shifted toward embodied AI, fueling rounds like Saronic's $1.75B Series D at a $9.25B valuation and Skild AI's $1.4B raise that tripled its valuation to over $14B.

title: WhatsApp head Will Cathcart is stepping down after seven years
blurb: Meta named Cred founder Kunal Shah as the new head of WhatsApp, replacing Will Cathcart after his seven-year run leading the messaging app. As part of the move, Meta is investing $900M into Shah's Indian fintech startup for a 20% stake, while Cathcart, who oversaw encrypted backups, ads, and iPad support, stays on at Meta in a new role.

title: Salesforce at 3.1x ARR, HubSpot Down 56%, Adobe at 11x Earnings: Are They Just Too Oversold Now?
blurb: Three iconic B2B leaders—Salesforce, HubSpot, and Adobe—kept sliding to fresh 52-week lows even as the broad software index rebounded more than 40% off its April bottom following February's $285B "SaaSpocalypse" selloff. The author argues the panic over AI agents gutting per-seat licensing has left them oversold, pointing to Salesforce trading near 3.1x ARR with 13% revenue growth and a record 34.8% operating margin.

title: The Doom Justifies the Valuation
blurb: George Hotz argues that AI labs lean on existential-risk messaging because their current technology cannot justify their valuations, contrasting Anthropic's exponential-danger framing with the plain technical writeups behind models like GLM-5.2. He claims doom narratives are optimized to provoke panic, sustaining a news cycle that prices the industry on hypothetical future value rather than what the products actually do today.

title: Google invests in independent movie studio A24 as part of a new AI partnership; sources say Google is investing $75M, equal to what Thrive invested in 2024 (Ben Fritz/Wall Street Journal)
blurb: Google invested $75M in independent film studio A24 as part of a new AI partnership, matching the amount Thrive Capital put in during 2024. The deal ties a frontier-AI player to a prestige Hollywood studio as generative tools push deeper into film production, signaling Google's intent to shape how AI gets used in creative work.

title: Getty Images Strikes Deal with OpenAI, Sending Getty’s Stock Soaring
blurb: Getty Images and OpenAI signed a multi-year partnership to display Getty's licensed visual content inside ChatGPT's search and discovery experiences. Getty's stock jumped more than 200% in premarket trading before settling around 123% up, though the companies disclosed no financial terms and few details on how OpenAI will use the imagery.

title: Microsoft turns to Chevron’s gas to power a Texas data centre
blurb: Microsoft signed a 20-year deal for Chevron to power a West Texas data center by burning natural gas, marking a sharp turn for a company that built its AI buildout on renewables and nuclear. Project Kilby near Pecos will run off-grid in the Permian Basin, delivering first power in 2028 and ramping to 2.67 gigawatts, making it one of the largest single sources of data-center power yet committed in the US.

title: SpaceX makes bond sale days after record IPO, discloses over $100 billion cash pile
blurb: SpaceX launched its inaugural senior unsecured notes offering and disclosed roughly $100.8 billion in cash, days after a record IPO that made Elon Musk the world's first trillionaire. The bond proceeds will retire bridge financing and fund the company's AI ambitions, including plans to build data centers in space, while shares fell about 8% on the news after briefly pushing SpaceX's market value past Amazon.

title: Dutch chip gear maker Nearfield Instruments raises $380M
blurb: Nearfield Instruments raised $380M in a Series D at a $1.6B valuation, led by Fidelity with Temasek, QIA, Walden Catalyst, and others, the largest deeptech round in Dutch history. The Rotterdam-based TNO spinout makes metrology and inspection tools that chipmakers use to control next-generation semiconductor production, and will spend the funding on R&D, innovation, and expanded manufacturing capacity.

title: A Nobel laureate just walked out of Google DeepMind for Anthropic, and the part nobody is discussing is what Alphabet had reassigned him to do before he left
blurb: John Jumper, who shared the 2024 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for AlphaFold, left Google DeepMind after nearly nine years to join Anthropic, where his protein-structure work shores up the lab's thin scientific credentials beyond language models. The exit, alongside Noam Shazeer's departure for OpenAI the same week, follows reports that DeepMind had reassigned Jumper from scientific research to its enterprise coding tools.

title: Google Investing in ‘Backrooms’ Studio A24
blurb: Google is investing about $75M in A24, the entertainment studio behind films like "Backrooms," as part of an AI research partnership. The deal pairs Google's AI capabilities with a major independent studio, signaling deeper tech-industry pushes into Hollywood content production and creative tooling.

title: GLM 5.2 vs. Opus
blurb: Open-weights GLM-5.2 ran a one-shot WebGL platformer against Claude Opus 4.8; Opus was faster and cleaner, GLM far cheaper.

title: Chevron signs a 20-year deal with Microsoft to provide natural-gas power for a proposed West Texas data center, which could be one of the biggest in the US (Kevin Crowley/Bloomberg)
blurb: Chevron signed a 20-year deal with Microsoft to supply natural-gas power for a proposed West Texas data center that could rank among the largest in the US. The agreement underscores how AI compute demand is pushing hyperscalers toward long-term, dedicated energy contracts, with gas filling the gap as grid capacity and renewables fall short of projected load.

title: Netherlands-based Nearfield Instruments, which makes atomic force microscopes to measure chip features, raised $380M led by Fidelity at a $1.6B valuation (Stephen Nellis/Reuters)
blurb: Nearfield Instruments, a Netherlands-based maker of atomic force microscopes used to measure nanoscale chip features, raised $380M led by Fidelity at a $1.6B valuation. The funding targets metrology tools that inspect increasingly dense semiconductor structures, a chokepoint as chipmakers push toward smaller process nodes and 3D architectures.

title: Nvidia Seeks to Make Humanoid AI Robots Safer Around Humans
blurb: Nvidia is developing safety systems to help humanoid robots make split-second decisions before they can be trusted to work in close proximity to people. The company argues that reliable real-time reaction handling is the prerequisite for deploying humanoids alongside human workers, positioning safety as the gating factor for the sector rather than raw capability.

title: Memory Safe Inline Assembly
blurb: Fil-C now supports GCC and clang inline assembly syntax while preserving complete memory safety, so mistakes like a missing clobber or constraint produce a panic or illegal-instruction trap instead of a silent miscompile. The feature is pre-release and not shipped in the 0.679 release, so testing it currently requires building Fil-C from source.

title: Tencent Tests AI Assistant for Its Super App WeChat in China
blurb: Tencent began testing an AI assistant inside WeChat, its billion-user super app, as it races to catch Chinese rivals like Alibaba and ByteDance in generative AI. The move embeds AI directly into a platform that already handles messaging, payments, and mini-programs for much of China, giving Tencent a built-in distribution channel few competitors can match.

title: California Needs Water and Clean Power. It Might Have a Fix for Both.
blurb: A California pilot program is installing solar panels over irrigation canals, generating clean electricity while the panel shade cuts water lost to evaporation. The dual-use approach sidesteps the land-acquisition fights that slow conventional solar farms, turning thousands of miles of existing canal infrastructure into power generation that also conserves a scarce resource.

title: Microsoft Weighs Hosting China's DeepSeek to Undercut OpenAI and Anthropic
blurb: Microsoft is weighing whether to host China's DeepSeek on its Copilot platform, routing customers to one of the cheapest models available and away from OpenAI and Anthropic. The move, reported by the WSJ, follows CEO Satya Nadella's pitch to commoditize frontier models and arrives amid weak Copilot uptake, under 4.5% of 450 million Microsoft 365 seats, despite the company's 27% OpenAI stake and up to $5B committed to Anthropic.

title: 2003-era DDR2 memory prices jump up to 60% — AI-driven DRAM shortage reaches the oldest standard still in production
blurb: DDR2 contract prices rose 55% to 60% in Q2 and are projected to climb another 35% to 40% in Q3, according to TrendForce, as the AI-driven memory shortage reaches the oldest DRAM standard still in production. With Samsung, SK hynix, and Micron steering wafers toward HBM and server DRAM, buyers are redesigning products around older memory, pushing shortages down through DDR4, DDR3, and now DDR2 parts used in embedded, automotive, and industrial systems.

title: Upscale, which is building AI networking infrastructure to rival Cisco, raised a $190M Series A-1 at a $2B valuation, up from $1B after raising $200M in January (Lily Mae Lazarus/Fortune)
blurb: Upscale raised a $190M Series A-1 at a $2B valuation to build AI networking infrastructure positioned against Cisco. The round doubles the startup's valuation from the $1B it hit in January after a $200M raise, signaling fast investor appetite for hardware that moves data between AI accelerators.

title: Anthropic Sued Over Claude Max Usage Limits Far Below Advertised Caps
blurb: A proposed class action filed in the Northern District of California alleges Anthropic's Claude Max plans deliver far less usage than advertised, claiming the $200 Max 20x tier provides only six to eight times Pro usage rather than the marketed 20 times. Plaintiff Karl Khan calls Anthropic's usage disclosures a black box and seeks over $5 million for U.S. customers who bought Max plans since April 2025.

title: SK Hynix overtakes Samsung to become South Korea's most valuable company, closing up 5.6% for a ~$1.4T market cap; South Korea's Kospi index is up 117% YTD (Kimberley Kao/Wall Street Journal)
blurb: SK Hynix overtook Samsung to become South Korea's most valuable company, closing up 5.6% for a market cap of roughly $1.4T. The memory-chip maker's surge tracks soaring demand for the high-bandwidth memory that powers AI accelerators, helping lift South Korea's Kospi index 117% year-to-date.

title: China Makes Sweeping Education Reforms to Prepare for AI Era
blurb: China eliminated over 12,000 university degree programs, dismissing many as outdated, in a sweeping overhaul meant to align higher education with the AI era. The move signals a state-directed bet that restructuring curricula now will determine which countries supply the next generation of AI-fluent workers.

title: SpaceX shares slide again as the post-IPO rally keeps unwinding
blurb: SpaceX shares closed last week near $185, down roughly 18% from the $225.64 they touched on June 16, unwinding much of the rally that followed the largest IPO in history. The slide tracks the start of put-options trading on SPCX and the company's $60B all-stock deal for Cursor maker Anysphere, which diluted recent buyers and sharpened questions about a valuation already pricing in about 100 times revenue.

title: Free Software Foundation Europe pushes EU to force Google to allow AI uninstalls on Android
blurb: The Free Software Foundation Europe asked the European Commission to force Google to let Android users fully uninstall preloaded AI components, citing models that silently reinstall after deletion in violation of the Digital Markets Act. The FSFE also wants interoperability decoupled from Google's developer verification, opposing the Android Developer Certification program launching September 2026, which would require every developer to register with Google before their apps can run on certified devices.

title: Pledging Another $400k to the Zig Software Foundation
blurb: Mitchell Hashimoto pledged another $400,000 to the Zig Software Foundation, bringing his family's total support to $700,000.

title: Ireland takes the EU presidency with Big Tech paying 40 per cent of its tax bill
blurb: Ireland takes over the rotating EU Council presidency on July 1 with an agenda packed with Big Tech regulation, despite an economy heavily dependent on those same firms. Apple and Microsoft alone paid roughly 40% of all Irish corporate tax in 2024, fueling conflict-of-interest concerns from MEPs and civil-liberties groups who want Ireland to recuse itself from digital files.

title: Microsoft’s Nadella turns on the AI giants he helped build
blurb: Microsoft CEO Satya Nadella told the Wall Street Journal that AI labs cannot promise to erase white-collar jobs while demanding unlimited power to build data centers, warning there is "no societal permission" for an AI future that hollows out entire industries. Aimed at OpenAI and Anthropic, the remarks back Microsoft's pitch for cheaper models and customer-controlled "learning loops" built on companies' own private data.

title: OpenAI signs deal to show Getty's images in ChatGPT results
blurb: Getty Images signed a multi-year deal with OpenAI to bring its licensed photo and video libraries into ChatGPT and OpenAI search results. The agreement marks a reversal for Getty, which banned AI-generated art in 2022 and sued Stability AI, and follows a similar October 2025 deal with Perplexity, though OpenAI has not disclosed whether the images can be used for model training.

title: Read this before you vibe-code another app
blurb: Vibe-coded apps are shipping with security holes like SQL injection, and the risk grows when private projects quietly start handling shared customer data.

title: Nearfield Instruments raises $380M to accelerate AI chipmaking
blurb: Nearfield Instruments raised $380M at a $1.6B valuation to scale chip metrology tools that inspect atomic-scale transistors during manufacturing.

title: Is China Closing the A.I. Gap Faster Than Expected?
blurb: Silicon Valley and corporate America are increasingly adopting cheaper, open-source AI models built in China, suggesting the capability gap with US frontier labs is narrowing faster than many expected. The shift is driven by cost rather than raw benchmark leadership, as open weights let companies self-host and fine-tune without paying per-token rates to OpenAI or Anthropic.

title: JD.com says robots will replace its 700,000 couriers
blurb: JD.com founder Richard Liu says robots will eventually replace all 700,000 of its couriers, a rare blunt admission from a tech boss.

title: NSA director: 'Mythos "broke into almost all of our classified systems in hours"
blurb: The NSA director said an AI system called Mythos broke into almost all of the agency's classified systems within hours during a security evaluation. The disclosure points to the growing offensive capability of autonomous AI in cyber operations and raises questions about whether existing government defenses can withstand machine-speed intrusion attempts.

title: Hundreds of AI-powered iOS apps found exposing credentials
blurb: Researchers at Wake Forest University analyzed 444 iOS apps with LLM features and found 282 exposing exploitable credentials or backend access, including authentication tokens, unauthenticated backends, and plaintext API keys. The leakage spans 13 app categories and affects popular titles, with 15% of vulnerable apps holding over 1,000 ratings and the largest carrying more than 2.3 million.

title: Sakana Fugu
blurb: Sakana AI released Fugu, an experiment in which an AI agent autonomously improved a small GPT's training recipe using AutoResearch, running 123 experiments over roughly 14 hours on a single H100. The agent's Fugu-Ultra configuration reached a best mean bits-per-byte of 0.9774, edging out three frontier-model baselines by tuning batch size, model depth, learning rates, and optimizer settings on its own.

title: Inside NVIDIA Halos for Robotics: A Full-Stack Functional Safety System for Physical AI
blurb: NVIDIA released Halos for Robotics, a full-stack functional safety platform that extends its autonomous-vehicle safety stack to industrial robots, humanoids, and autonomous mobile robots. The system pairs NVIDIA IGX Thor hardware with Halos OS and aligns to standards like ISO 13849 and IEC 61508, with Agility Robotics adopting it for safe human detection in its Digit humanoid.

title: 23 ClawHub plugins squatting official scopes expose AI registry security gaps
blurb: Researchers found 23 code-executing plugins published under ClawHub's official @openclaw and @clawhub scopes while owned by unrelated accounts, exposing a scope-squatting gap in the AI plugin registry. The plugins run with Claude, OpenClaw, and other agents, making an official-looking scope a supply chain risk even when the code is benign, and ClawHub reserved the affected scopes after the disclosure.

title: Codex logging bug may write TBs to local SSDs
blurb: A GitHub issue reports that OpenAI's Codex writes ~640 TB per year to local SSDs through its SQLite feedback logs, with one user measuring 37 TB written over 21 days of uptime. TRACE-level logging accounts for roughly 71% of retained bytes, and the writes could exhaust a consumer SSD's rated 600 TBW endurance in under a year.

title: Nvidia unveils Halos, a safety-focused OS developed from autonomous vehicle tech and designed to run on IGX Thor hardware for humanoid robots, and opens a lab (Ian King/Bloomberg)
blurb: Nvidia introduced Halos, a safety-focused operating system adapted from its autonomous vehicle stack and built to run on IGX Thor hardware for humanoid robots. The company also opened a dedicated lab to develop the technology, extending its self-driving safety work into the emerging market for general-purpose robots.

title: Samsung Electronics brings ChatGPT and Codex to employees
blurb: Samsung Electronics deployed ChatGPT Enterprise and Codex to its employees worldwide, in one of OpenAI's largest enterprise rollouts to date. The agreement puts conversational AI and coding assistance in the hands of staff across Samsung's global operations, spanning everything from chip design and manufacturing to consumer electronics teams.

title: Getty Images Soars 200% in Early Trading After OpenAI Deal
blurb: Getty Images shares jumped roughly 200% in premarket trading after the stock-photo company announced a content licensing deal with OpenAI. The agreement signals OpenAI's continued push to secure rights-cleared training and reference imagery as legal scrutiny over AI data sourcing intensifies.

title: How Claude Mythos found a 15-year-old bug in Mozilla Firefox | Brian Grinstead
blurb: Mozilla distinguished engineer Brian Grinstead explains how his team pointed an agentic bug-finding pipeline at Firefox, a codebase with tens of millions of lines, and shipped a record month of security fixes including one 15-year-old bug. He argues the harness deserves roughly half the credit alongside Anthropic's Mythos model, detailing a goal-loop pattern where an LLM judge ranks files, a verifier subagent kills false positives, and humans still review every patch.

title: PP-OCRv6 on Hugging Face: 50-Language OCR from 1.5M to 34.5M Parameters
blurb: PP-OCRv6 is PaddleOCR's latest OCR model family on Hugging Face, scaling from 1.5M to 34.5M parameters across tiny, small, and medium tiers, with the small and medium tiers covering 50 languages including Simplified Chinese, Japanese, and 46 Latin-script languages. On PaddleOCR's in-house benchmarks, the medium tier reaches 86.2% detection Hmean and 83.2% recognition accuracy, improving on PP-OCRv5_server by 4.6 and 5.1 percentage points.

title: In a rare joint statement, Five Eyes leaders warn AI models capable of taking down governments and businesses are mere months away, urging leaders to "act now" (Sarah Basford Canales/The Guardian)
blurb: Five Eyes intelligence leaders issued a rare joint statement warning that AI models capable of crippling governments and businesses are only months away, urging policymakers to act now. The alliance, which spans the US, UK, Canada, Australia, and New Zealand, called for immediate coordination on safeguards rather than waiting for the capabilities to arrive.

title: Agent Beacon: Open-source telemetry layer for AI agents
blurb: Asymptote Labs released Agent Beacon, an open-source telemetry layer that configures runtimes like Claude Code, Codex CLI, and Cursor to export OpenTelemetry data and writes a normalized JSON log of agent file edits, commands, and tool calls across local, CI, and cloud surfaces. Written in Go under the MIT license, it runs locally without an account, binds its dashboard to loopback, and supports configurable retention modes plus forwarding to customer-managed SIEMs.

title: 잘못된 Claude.md가 AI를 망친다…연구진이 꼽은 설정 파일 6대 문제
blurb: Researchers at the Federal University of Minas Gerais proposed the first catalog of "configuration smells" in coding-agent files like Agents.md and Claude.md, identifying six recurring defects that bloat context, waste tokens, and degrade reliability. Across 100 popular open-source repositories, 91 contained at least one smell, with lint leakage appearing in 62%, context bloat in 42%, and skill leakage in 35%.

title: Google invests in independent movie studio A24 as part of a new AI partnership; sources say Google is investing $75M, equal to what Thrive invested in 2024 (Ben Fritz/Wall Street Journal)
blurb: Google invested $75M in independent movie studio A24 as part of a new AI partnership. The figure matches the amount Thrive Capital invested in the studio in 2024, and the deal signals Google's push to embed its AI tools within entertainment production workflows.

title: The Doom Justifies the Valuation
blurb: George Hotz argues that AI labs lean on doom and existential-risk framing because the current technology doesn't justify their valuations. He contrasts the GLM-5.2 release post, a technical look at incremental progress on a model he rates on par with Opus 4.8 and GPT-5.5, with Anthropic's posts about exponential acceleration and recursive self-improvement, which he reads as hype optimized to induce panic rather than describe a product.

title: AI 도입 넘어 비용 관리로…오픈AI, 사용량 분석·예산 설정 기능 공개
blurb: OpenAI added spending controls and expanded usage analytics to ChatGPT Enterprise, giving admins a centralized dashboard to monitor adoption, track per-team consumption, and set AI budgets. A new Global Admin Console breaks down ChatGPT and Codex credit usage by user, product, and model, though analysts note the tools still measure activity rather than tie AI spending to actual business outcomes.

title: Getty Images soars after striking a deal with the kind of company it sued
blurb: Getty Images announced a multi-year display partnership with OpenAI to surface its licensed photo libraries across ChatGPT's search and discovery features, and its shares jumped roughly 200% in premarket trading. The deal covers display only, with no disclosed financial terms and no provision for training future models, the same activity Getty is still litigating against Stability AI after a UK court largely rejected its copyright claim.

title: Import AI 462: Superpersuasion; self-sustaining AI; paths to ASI
blurb: A study from Oxford, the UK AI Security Institute, Stanford, and LSE found AI systems are already more persuasive than expert humans at text-based persuasion with real stakes. Across four experiments spanning 18,978 conversations with 6,923 people, AI outperformed even coached elite debaters and was nearly 3x more effective than professional canvassers at raising real donations to Save the Children. The edge came from rapidly deploying more information; constraining AI to human speed and message length erased it.

title: Getty Images Strikes Deal with OpenAI, Sending Getty’s Stock Soaring
blurb: Getty Images and OpenAI announced a multi-year display partnership to surface Getty's licensed visual content inside ChatGPT's search and discovery experiences. Getty's stock jumped more than 200% in premarket trading, though the announcement disclosed no financial terms, no definition of "multi-year," and no detail on how OpenAI will use the images.

title: Getty Images signs a licensing deal with OpenAI, letting its image library appear in ChatGPT's search and discovery tools; GETY jumps 90%+ and is down 10%+ YTD (Mark Bergen/Bloomberg)
blurb: Getty Images signed a content licensing deal with OpenAI, allowing its image library to surface within ChatGPT's search and discovery features. Getty's stock jumped more than 90% on the news, though shares remain down over 10% year to date, underscoring how much ground the company had lost before the agreement.

title: Google Investing in ‘Backrooms’ Studio A24
blurb: Google is investing about $75 million in A24, the studio behind "The Backrooms," as part of an AI research partnership. The deal pairs Google's AI capabilities with the entertainment company's filmmaking work, signaling deeper ties between frontier labs and Hollywood production.

title: Chevron signs a 20-year deal with Microsoft to provide natural-gas power for a proposed West Texas data center, which could be one of the biggest in the US (Kevin Crowley/Bloomberg)
blurb: Chevron signed a 20-year deal with Microsoft to supply natural-gas power for a proposed data center in West Texas, which could become one of the largest in the US. The agreement reflects how AI compute demand is pushing hyperscalers toward long-term energy contracts to secure dedicated power for new facilities.

title: Good results fine tuning a local LLM like Qwen 3:0.6B to categorize questions
blurb: This post tests whether a 600M-parameter local model, Qwen 3:0.6B, can be fine-tuned into a reliable question classifier for a metadata-aware RAG chatbot. Using the Unsloth framework on ~850 household questions split 70/15/15, the author categorizes queries into known buckets like pool, car, and HVAC to narrow the vector search space before retrieval.

title: Show HN: Recall – Local project memory for Claude Code
blurb: Recall is a local memory plugin for Claude Code that logs each session to an append-only history file and condenses it into a compact context.md summary using a classical Python summarizer rather than an LLM call. It solves the cold-start problem without spending model tokens or sending transcripts to any external API, working offline the moment the plugin loads.

title: GLM 5.2 vs. Opus
blurb: Z.ai's open-weights GLM-5.2 ran behind Claude Opus 4.8 on a one-shot WebGL platformer test but cost a fraction as much.

title: Nvidia Seeks to Make Humanoid AI Robots Safer Around Humans
blurb: Nvidia is developing safety systems for humanoid robots, arguing they must handle split-second decisions before working closely with people.

title: Anthropic Sued Over Claude Max Usage Limits Far Below Advertised Caps
blurb: A Claude subscriber filed a proposed class action against Anthropic, alleging its Max plans deliver far less usage than advertised. The complaint says the $200-a-month Max 20x tier provides only six to eight times the usage of Pro rather than the marketed 20 times, calls Anthropic's usage disclosures a black box, and seeks over $5M for U.S. Max buyers since April 2025.

title: There is minimal downside to switching to open models
blurb: The author argues that switching from proprietary models like Claude and GPT to open models now carries minimal downside, drawing a parallel to Linux shedding its professional-risk reputation. While proprietary models still top intelligence leaderboards and offer cleaner APIs and stronger privacy guarantees, the post contends that gap is narrowing fast, with Claude's new ID verification rollout pushing the author to make the move for professional work.

title: A Nobel laureate just walked out of Google DeepMind for Anthropic, and the part nobody is discussing is what Alphabet had reassigned him to do before he left
blurb: John Jumper, who shared the 2024 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for AlphaFold, left Google DeepMind for Anthropic after nearly nine years, marking the lab's most prominent scientific hire beyond language models. Reports indicate Alphabet had reassigned Jumper from scientific discovery to its enterprise coding tools before his exit, which came the same week Noam Shazeer departed DeepMind for OpenAI.

title: Microsoft Weighs Hosting China's DeepSeek to Undercut OpenAI and Anthropic
blurb: Microsoft is weighing whether to host China's DeepSeek on its Copilot platform, a move that would steer customers toward one of the cheapest available models and away from OpenAI and Anthropic. CEO Satya Nadella framed the plan as an effort to commoditize frontier models, even as Microsoft keeps a 27% stake in OpenAI and up to $5B committed to Anthropic.

title: OpenAI signs deal to show Getty's images in ChatGPT results
blurb: Getty Images signed a multi-year partnership with OpenAI to bring its licensed visual content libraries into OpenAI search and ChatGPT results. The deal reverses Getty's earlier hard line against AI companies, which included a 2022 ban on AI-generated art and a copyright suit against Stability AI, though Getty has not disclosed whether its images can be used for model training.

title: NYC-based Prosper AI, whose health care AI tool is designed to answer patient phone calls, raised a $30M Series A led by a16z, for $35M in total funding (Brock E.W. Turner/Axios)
blurb: Prosper AI, a NYC-based startup whose health care tool answers patient phone calls, raised a $30M Series A led by a16z, bringing total funding to $35M. The product targets the high volume of routine patient calls that strain provider front-office staff, automating intake and scheduling tasks typically handled by humans.

title: Nearfield Instruments raises $380M to accelerate AI chipmaking
blurb: Nearfield Instruments raised $380M in a Series D round led by Fidelity, valuing the Dutch chip metrology company at $1.6B in what it called the largest round ever raised by a Dutch firm. The Rotterdam-based company builds 3D metrology and process control systems that inspect atomic-scale transistor structures during fabrication, a bottleneck as chipmakers move to gate-all-around and CFET architectures.

title: Is China Closing the A.I. Gap Faster Than Expected?
blurb: Silicon Valley and corporate America are increasingly adopting cheaper, open-source AI models built in China, signaling that the capability gap with US labs may be narrowing faster than anticipated. The shift is driven by cost, as Chinese open-weight models offer comparable performance for many tasks without the licensing fees and API expenses tied to frontier US systems.

title: Getty Images Soars 200% in Early Trading After OpenAI Deal
blurb: Getty Images jumped about 200% in premarket trading after announcing a licensing deal with OpenAI. The same company that sued Stability AI for training on its images without permission is now getting paid by a frontier lab for legitimate access. The market read is that licensed content libraries are an asset, not roadkill, as labs increasingly pay for clean training data rather than risk the lawsuits.

title: Meta Taps New WhatsApp Boss as Part of $900 Million Investment
blurb: Meta is investing $900 million into Indian fintech Cred and installing its founder, Kunal Shah, as the new head of WhatsApp. The move ties Meta's biggest messaging app to a payments operator at the moment WhatsApp is trying to turn India, its largest market, into a commerce and fintech engine rather than a free chat app. Putting a fintech founder in charge signals where Meta thinks the next decade of WhatsApp revenue comes from, and it puts every startup building payments on top of WhatsApp on notice.

title: Meta invests $900M into Indian fintech Cred for a ~20% stake, and plans to appoint Cred founder Kunal Shah as the leader of WhatsApp, replacing Will Cathcart (Kurt Wagner/Bloomberg)
blurb: Meta invested $900M into Indian fintech Cred for roughly a 20% stake and plans to install Cred founder Kunal Shah atop WhatsApp, replacing Will Cathcart. The deal reads less like a fintech bet than a leadership grab, buying Meta a payments-native operator to run its messaging platform as WhatsApp pushes deeper into commerce. India is WhatsApp's largest market, and handing it to a founder who built one of the country's most-watched payments products signals where Meta thinks the app's next revenue actually comes from.

title: Apple Price Increases, Apple Intelligence and the E.U.
blurb: Apple is finally raising prices on its hardware, but it is withholding the new Siri and Apple Intelligence features from the E.U. The split traces back to the bloc's regulatory regime, which Apple treats as too risky a place to ship its most integrated AI, so European customers pay more for less capable devices. For founders, it is the clearest sign yet that the E.U.'s rules are pushing the largest platforms to ration features by geography rather than ship everywhere at once.

title: Getty Images signs a licensing deal with OpenAI, letting its image library appear in ChatGPT's search and discovery tools; GETY jumps 90%+ and is down 10%+ YTD (Mark Bergen/Bloomberg)
blurb: Getty Images signed a licensing deal with OpenAI, putting its image library inside ChatGPT's search and discovery tools. The stock jumped more than 90% on the news even though GETY is still down over 10% on the year, a sign of how much investors are pricing into any direct revenue line from the AI labs. The deal also marks a shift for Getty, which has sued other AI companies over training data and is now choosing to license its catalog into the products built on top of that data.

title: Getty Images soars after striking a deal with the kind of company it sued
blurb: Getty Images shares jumped about 200% in premarket trading after the company signed a multi-year deal to surface its licensed photos inside ChatGPT's search and discovery features. The pivot is striking because Getty spent years suing image generators like Stability AI for training on its catalog, and largely lost in the UK High Court. The deal covers display, not training, and the release disclosed no financial terms or revenue split. The smart read is a company hedging in public: keep litigating where you were wronged, sign where you can get paid.

title: Crypto trading app Fomo raises $75 million at $550 million valuation in Index Ventures-led Series B
blurb: Fomo, a crypto trading app, raised $75M at a $550M valuation in a Series B led by Index Ventures, with USV and others participating. The round lands as consumer crypto trading rebounds and investors compete to back the next retail-facing brokerage rather than another infrastructure play. A $550M mark on a Series B signals how aggressively capital is chasing distribution and active traders in the space.

title: Sector Snapshot: Robotics Startups On Fire As Venture Funding Surges To Record Numbers In 2026
blurb: Robotics startups have raised $18.8B globally in 2026 so far, already past 2025's full-year $15B and the $14.1B peak of 2021, with more than six months left to go. The money is chasing embodied AI, the bet that put physical bodies on models, and it has flipped venture's old read of robotics as an asset-heavy hardware gamble. Saronic's $1.75B Series D, Skild AI tripling to a $14B valuation, and Neura's $1.4B round show the checks now rival anything in software, with two of the five biggest raises coming out of Austin.

title: Google invests in independent movie studio A24 as part of a new AI partnership; sources say Google is investing $75M, equal to what Thrive invested in 2024 (Ben Fritz/Wall Street Journal)
blurb: Google invested $75M in A24 as part of a new AI partnership, matching the amount Thrive put in during 2024. The deal pairs a frontier AI lab with one of the most distinctive independent studios in film, signaling that generative tools are moving toward prestige content rather than just cheap volume. For founders, it's another sign the labs are buying their way into creative workflows instead of waiting for the industry to adopt them.

title: Seedcamp raises $320M for its new fund to expand its US footprint
blurb: Seedcamp raised $320M for Fund VII, its largest ever and double the $180M it closed in 2023, to push deeper into the US after 18 years centered on Europe. The firm is splitting the money, with $220M for early-stage first checks of around $1M into 100 to 120 startups and a new $100M Select fund writing $3M to $5M growth-stage follow-ons. The move signals that European seed investors now see San Francisco as the connective node their portfolios need, not just a market to sell into.

title: The Doom Justifies the Valuation
blurb: George Hotz argues that frontier labs lean on apocalyptic AI rhetoric because the technology itself no longer justifies the valuation. He contrasts GLM-5.2's plain technical writeup, a model on par with Opus 4.8 and GPT-5.5, with Anthropic's posts about exponential risk and recursive self-improvement, and reads the doom framing as optimized to sustain a story of infinite future value. The claim is that hype substitutes for product when the current capability can't carry the price.

title: Salesforce at 3.1x ARR, HubSpot Down 56%, Adobe at 11x Earnings: Are They Just Too Oversold Now?
blurb: Four months after the "SaaSpocalypse" wiped roughly $285B in software market cap in 48 hours, the broad index has ripped back more than 40% off its April low. Salesforce, HubSpot, and Adobe didn't come with it, each sliding to fresh 52-week lows on the fear that AI agents kill per-seat licensing. The numbers complicate that thesis: Salesforce just posted 13% revenue growth, a record 34.8% operating margin, and 50% EPS growth while trading near 3x ARR. AI revenue is real but hasn't yet driven net growth, which is exactly the test these franchises have to pass.

title: Rippling’s AI Bet: The Data Graph Is the Moat
blurb: Rippling's AI bet rests on one structural choice: a single connected database under all 25+ products, built in-house rather than acquired. The argument is that AI can only do real work on one clean graph, where the hard part isn't the million queryable fields but the layer on top that maps relationships, enforces permissions, and picks which fields answer the question. The product arc that follows is the right one for anyone building AI on business data: insights first, then actions, then proactive workflows.

title: SpaceX makes bond sale days after record IPO, discloses over $100 billion cash pile
blurb: SpaceX raised nearly $86 billion in its June 12 IPO, then days later launched an inaugural bond offering targeting about $20 billion, while disclosing a roughly $100.8 billion cash pile. The IPO briefly pushed its market value past Amazon and made Musk the world's first trillionaire, but shares fell about 8% on the bond news. The proceeds go toward bridge financing and SpaceX's AI ambitions, which include eventually building data centers in space, a sign that even a company sitting on $100 billion is raising debt to fund the capex race.

title: Microsoft turns to Chevron’s gas to power a Texas data centre
blurb: Microsoft signed a 20-year deal with Chevron to power one of the largest US data centres entirely off-grid, burning natural gas from the Permian Basin instead of drawing from the Texas grid. Project Kilby will ramp to 2.67 gigawatts near Pecos by 2028, roughly enough for 2 million homes, fed by the basin's flared byproduct gas that keeps local prices low. For a company that built its AI roadmap on renewables and nuclear, the turn to fossil fuels shows that the binding constraint on AI buildout is now firm power, not climate commitments.

title: Dutch chip gear maker Nearfield Instruments raises $380M
blurb: Nearfield Instruments, a Rotterdam spinout from research institute TNO, raised $380M at a $1.6B valuation in the largest deeptech round in Dutch history, led by Fidelity with Temasek, QIA, and Walden Catalyst joining. The company makes metrology and inspection tools that let chipmakers control fabrication at the precision the next semiconductor generation demands. The round more than doubles its July 2025 raise of $148M, a sign that the capital chasing AI compute is now flowing down into the unglamorous European hardware that makes advanced chips possible.

title: SpaceX Kicks Off Debut High-Grade Bond Sale
blurb: SpaceX is selling investment-grade bonds for the first time, tapping the high-grade debt market to fund its AI ambitions on the back of a record IPO. The move signals that even the most cash-rich frontier players now see borrowing as the cheaper way to finance the compute buildout. When a company that just went public at record scale still reaches for debt, it tells you how expensive the AI infrastructure race has become.

title: Netherlands-based Nearfield Instruments, which makes atomic force microscopes to measure chip features, raised $380M led by Fidelity at a $1.6B valuation (Stephen Nellis/Reuters)
blurb: Nearfield Instruments, the Dutch maker of atomic force microscopes used to measure features on advanced chips, raised $380M led by Fidelity at a $1.6B valuation. The round is a bet that metrology, not just lithography, becomes a chokepoint as chipmakers push to smaller nodes and need to inspect structures too fine for older tools to see. The capital is flowing into the unglamorous middle of the semiconductor stack, where a handful of specialized European suppliers already hold near-monopolies that the AI buildout keeps making more valuable.

title: Seedcamp, Europe’s original ‘first cheque’ investor, raises $320m
blurb: Seedcamp raised $320M for its seventh fund, splitting $220M into its flagship first-cheque vehicle and $100M into a Select fund that follows winners toward Series B. The firm that wrote Europe's earliest checks into Revolut, Wise, and Synthesia now manages over $1B and is pairing its early-stage book with an expanded US team to pull portfolio companies transatlantic from day one. The bet for Fund VII is AI and hard tech with applications in science and the physical world, signaled by recent deals in space manufacturing, autonomous robotics, and AI agents.

title: European Investor Seedcamp Closes On $320M Across Two Funds To Back Seed Startups And Reaches $1B AUM
blurb: Seedcamp closed two new funds totaling $320M, a $220M seventh core fund and a $100M select fund, pushing the European seed investor past $1B in assets under management since its $3M start in 2007. The firm plans 35 new investments a year at checks up to $1.3M, leading roughly 70% of deals for 5% to 10% ownership, and is steering away from crowded AI verticals toward defense, health, and robotics. Its edge is a portfolio community where founders just slightly ahead become the most useful help for the next cohort.

title: Robinhood Seeks to Raise $2 Billion in Convertible Bond Sale
blurb: Robinhood is seeking to raise $2B through a convertible bond offering, joining a wave of companies tapping the market to stockpile cash. Convertibles let it raise capital at lower coupons than straight debt by handing investors upside if the stock climbs, deferring dilution until conversion. The rush into these instruments signals that even profitable, cash-generative firms now want a war chest on the balance sheet while financing is cheap.

title: Investing in Prosper AI
blurb: a16z led Prosper AI's Series A, betting that the next layer of healthcare software is the office phone, not another patient portal. Prosper runs clinic operations end-to-end, scheduling, insurance eligibility, and patient billing, and it is built for the combinatorial mess of large specialty groups with 10+ locations, 100+ appointment types, and 1000+ insurance plan combinations. In under two years it has signed leading specialty practices and channel deals with major EHR and RCM vendors that put it in front of hundreds of thousands of providers, with revenue compounding as scheduled and paid appointments climb.

title: ‘Owning and controlling the entire AI stack is essential’: Europe is doubling down on its own AI infrastructure
blurb: Europe is building its own AI hardware stack as tech sovereignty becomes a national-security priority, not a slogan. European AI hardware and compute startups have raised €1.9B already this year, nearly matching the €2B raised in all of 2025, with London's Nscale landing a $2B Series C at a $14.6B valuation and Dutch neocloud Nebius closing $4.3B. The driver is dependence: three US hyperscalers control 70% of Europe's cloud market, so the UK is committing £1.1B to homegrown infrastructure and the European Commission has proposed a Tech Sovereignty Package to cut reliance on foreign chips and data centers.

title: Upscale, which is building AI networking infrastructure to rival Cisco, raised a $190M Series A-1 at a $2B valuation, up from $1B after raising $200M in January (Lily Mae Lazarus/Fortune)
blurb: Upscale raised a $190M Series A-1 at a $2B valuation to build AI networking infrastructure aimed at Cisco, doubling its worth from $1B just five months after a $200M round in January. The pace signals where capital is flowing: not the model labs, but the unglamorous plumbing that has to move the traffic AI workloads generate. Investors are betting that the data-center buildout creates a hardware layer big enough to challenge incumbents who have owned enterprise networking for decades.

title: NYC-based Prosper AI, whose health care AI tool is designed to answer patient phone calls, raised a $30M Series A led by a16z, for $35M in total funding (Brock E.W. Turner/Axios)
blurb: Prosper AI raised a $30M Series A led by a16z, bringing the New York healthcare startup to $35M in total funding for a tool that answers patient phone calls. The bet is that the unglamorous front desk, not the diagnosis, is where AI gets adopted in healthcare first, because phone triage is high-volume, rules-bound, and the cost of a missed call is concrete. a16z leading the round signals that voice agents aimed at a single vertical workflow are now drawing top-tier capital, while horizontal assistants still struggle to prove ROI.

title: SK Hynix overtakes Samsung to become South Korea's most valuable company, closing up 5.6% for a ~$1.4T market cap; South Korea's Kospi index is up 117% YTD (Kimberley Kao/Wall Street Journal)
blurb: SK Hynix passed Samsung to become South Korea's most valuable company, closing up 5.6% for a market cap near $1.4T as the memory maker rides demand for the high-bandwidth chips that feed AI training. The move tracks a broader rerating of the picks-and-shovels layer of the AI buildout, where component suppliers are capturing value faster than the model labs. South Korea's Kospi is up 117% year to date, a reminder that the largest AI gains are increasingly accruing outside US tech.

title: Amazon Prime Day Spending Expected to Rise 9% to $26.3 Billion
blurb: Amazon Prime Day spending is expected to climb 9% to a record $26.3 billion during the 12th annual sale starting Tuesday, driven by shoppers hunting back-to-school clothes and household goods. The demand is showing up despite higher gas prices squeezing budgets, which is pushing people toward discounts rather than away from spending. For anyone selling online, the event is now a fixed mid-year demand spike that sets the pace for the rest of the retail calendar.

title: SpaceX Declines for a Third Day After Announcing Bond Sale
blurb: SpaceX is selling investment-grade bonds for the first time, and its shares have now fallen three days straight on the news. The bond sale is the opening move in what's expected to be a heavy borrowing spree to fund the company's AI ambitions. A company that long ran on equity and government contracts taking on debt for compute marks how far the capital demands of AI now reach, even for the most cash-generative private companies.

title: Chinese Backing Helps African Startup Spiro Near Unicorn Status
blurb: Chinese investor NewTrails Capital is putting $55M into Spiro, an African electric-mobility startup, pushing it close to a $1B valuation. The deal extends a pattern of Chinese capital backing African EV and battery-swapping ventures as Western venture money stays scarce on the continent. For founders, it signals where the next pool of growth capital is forming: emerging-market hardware plays funded by Chinese strategics rather than Silicon Valley.

title: SpaceX shares slide again as the post-IPO rally keeps unwinding
blurb: SpaceX has given back most of its post-IPO pop, closing around $185 last week, down roughly 18% from the $225.64 peak it hit four days after listing. The move is mechanical, not fundamental: only about 4% of shares float freely until lockups start easing near summer earnings, so thin volume swung the stock 67% up and now back down. The sharper signal is strategic, as SpaceX spent $60bn of freshly public stock to buy Cursor maker Anysphere days after listing, diluting new holders and telling them management is betting the growth on AI.

title: EU Inc. could fix one of Europe’s biggest startup headaches, but only if done right
blurb: Europe's biggest startup tax isn't talent, it's its own paperwork. A growth-stage company expanding market by market stacks duplicated entities, country-specific holding structures, and inconsistent stock-option rules until it becomes easier to scale into the US than across European borders. EU Inc., the proposed 28th corporate regime, would fix the worst of this with an optional EU-wide status: 48-hour formation capped at €100, full digitalization, and automatic recognition across Member States. The catch is that it only works if treated as a foundation, not a silver bullet.

title: COMAND AI lève 32 millions d’euros : la défense européenne ne vise plus la souveraineté, mais la suprématie
blurb: Comand AI raised €32M in a Series A led by Blossom Capital, with Saab taking a strategic stake, to scale Prevail, its AI command-and-control platform already deployed with units in France, Germany, and Ukraine. The round signals a shift in how European defense founders pitch: the goal is no longer a sovereign alternative to US software but setting the global standard for military command systems. Alongside Helsing, Quantum Systems, and Tekever, this cohort is reframing European defense from strategic autonomy to industrial supremacy, with Ukraine as the proving ground that changed investor appetite.

title: Seedcamp raises €279 million to back Europe’s next generation of global startups
blurb: Seedcamp raised €279 million ($320 million) for Fund VII, splitting it into a €192 million first-check fund and an €87 million Select fund to follow winners into Series B and beyond. The early backer of Revolut, Wise, and UiPath now manages over €1 billion, with Fund III tracking to a 20x net return. The pitch this time is a Transatlantic Bridge: an expanded US team bringing American capital, hires, and customers to European founders, with recent bets like space manufacturer BioOrbit and AI agent company Dust signaling a tilt toward AI and the physical world.

title: ElevationSpace Closes $40M Series B
blurb: ElevationSpace, the Japanese satellite reentry startup, raised a $40M Series B, pushing total funding to $63.5M and signaling that reentry and recovery is becoming its own venture category alongside launch. The company is building uncrewed platforms for in-orbit research and high-frequency payload returns, with its first reentry satellite, AOBA, set to launch later this year on Isar Aerospace's Spectrum rocket. The bet is that returning materials from orbit, crewed stations, and eventually Mars is the missing piece of space transportation infrastructure, and partnerships with Axiom Space and Redwire suggest the market agrees.

title: Why Meta wants CRED: The $4B fintech deal that could reshape India’s payments industry
blurb: Meta is reportedly in talks to invest in Bengaluru-based fintech CRED at a roughly $4B valuation, putting tens of millions of dollars in as primary capital, though the talks are exploratory and unresolved. The pieces Meta lacks in India are payments, and CRED would bolt financial infrastructure plus one of the country's most affluent user bases onto Facebook, Instagram, and WhatsApp. The catch is that PhonePe and Google Pay already control nearly 80% of UPI transactions, so buying into CRED is a bet on owning the high-income tier rather than winning the volume war.

title: Ireland takes the EU presidency with Big Tech paying 40 per cent of its tax bill
blurb: Ireland takes over the EU Council presidency on 1 July with an agenda full of tech regulation, even though its economy runs on the companies those rules target. Sixteen of the world's 20 largest tech firms operate hubs in Ireland, and just two of them, understood to be Apple and Microsoft, paid almost 40% of all Irish corporate tax in 2024, roughly €11 billion. The presidency is meant to be an honest broker among 27 states, but civil-liberties groups are already calling for Ireland to recuse itself from every digital file.

title: Microsoft’s Nadella turns on the AI giants he helped build
blurb: Satya Nadella, who helped start the AI boom, is now warning the labs running it that there is no societal permission for an AI future that hollows out entire industries. His argument is that you cannot promise the end of white-collar work while demanding limitless power to build data centers, because the political economy will not tolerate value accruing to a few models that eat everything they see. Microsoft's counter-pitch is a frontier ecosystem rather than a single frontier model, where every company builds its own learning loop from private data and cheaper, more controllable models.

title: China Targets US Rare Earths Firms in Response to Pentagon
blurb: China placed two US rare earth producers on its export control list, both central to Washington's push to build a supply chain independent of Beijing. The move targets the exact firms meant to break China's grip, signaling it will pressure the alternative chain before it can scale. For founders sourcing magnets, batteries, or chips, it is a reminder that critical-mineral supply is now a live geopolitical variable, not a fixed cost line.

title: SEEDCAMP lève 279 millions d’euros : pourquoi la prochaine vague technologique européenne ne ressemblera pas à la précédente
blurb: Seedcamp raised €279M for its seventh fund, betting that Europe's next generation of champions won't look like Revolut, Wise, or UiPath. The firm that backed the first wave of European software now argues that cycle is maturing, and the value is shifting to where AI meets science and the physical world. Its recent checks point the way, with bets on space-based manufacturing, autonomous robotics, and AI agents rather than another SaaS or fintech.

title: Lucid to lay off roughly 18% of U.S. workforce, COO Marc Winterhoff leaves
blurb: Lucid is cutting roughly 18% of its U.S. workforce, eliminating the COO role and dropping the second production shift at its Arizona plant, for about $158M in annualized savings. The moves follow new CEO Silvio Napoli suspending guidance to clear elevated inventory, which for automakers usually means idling production. The deeper signal is the burn: Lucid lost $2.7B on $1.35B of revenue last year, with negative free cash flow of $3.8B, so aligning output to demand is now a survival cut rather than a growth lever.

title: Electric bike rental company Lime is seeking to raise up to $180.9M in its US IPO, offering 6.7M shares at $24 to $26 each, giving it an up to $1.7B valuation (Jordan Fitzgerald/Bloomberg)
blurb: Lime filed to raise up to $180.9M in its US IPO, offering 6.7M shares at $24 to $26 each for a valuation of up to $1.7B. The micromobility company is testing whether public markets still have appetite for asset-heavy, hardware-dependent businesses after years where software multiples set the bar. For founders watching the IPO window, Lime's pricing is a read on how patient capital values steady unit economics over the hypergrowth story that dominated the last cycle.

title: Uber anchors Lime’s IPO as the scooter firm goes public
blurb: Lime is taking Neutron Holdings public on the Nasdaq under LIME, seeking up to $180.9M at $24 to $26 a share for a valuation near $1.7B, with Uber stepping in as anchor investor. The tie is structural: Uber already owns more than 10% of Lime, guarantees some of its debt, and routes about 14% of Lime's revenue through its app. Lime reaches the public markets as one of the last venture-backed names left in micromobility after VanMoof and Rad Power collapsed, though its net loss still widened to $59.3M on $886.7M in 2025 revenue.

title: Onfido founder raises $40M to kill the 12-month certification bottleneck holding back the industrial economy
blurb: Eamon Jubbawy, who scaled Onfido to over a billion identity checks before its $650M sale to Entrust, raised $40M in a Series A led by AVP for Isometric, an AI platform that compresses industrial certification from 12 months to hours. The bet is that the same fix he ran on identity verification, replacing slow human sampling with machines reading every data point, applies to the $350B certification market BCG estimates AI has barely touched. After contracting 16M tonnes of carbon removal for customers like Microsoft and Boeing, Isometric is now walking that model into the broader industrial economy.

title: Hundreds of AI-powered iOS apps found exposing credentials
blurb: Researchers at Wake Forest University analyzed 444 iOS apps with LLM features and found 282, or 64%, exposed exploitable credentials, including 54 plaintext API keys and 136 leaked authentication tokens. The most popular affected app had over 2.3 million ratings, and 55% of leaks routed traffic through custom developer backends, meaning provider-side fixes alone cannot close the gap.

title: Deno Desktop
blurb: Deno v2.9.0 introduces `deno desktop`, a command that turns any Deno project from a single TypeScript file to a Next.js, SvelteKit, or Vite SSR app into a self-contained, cross-compilable binary bundling the runtime and a web rendering engine. It defaults to the OS webview for small binaries with optional bundled Chromium, swaps socket IPC for in-process bindings, and ships built-in bsdiff auto-update, though it currently runs only in the canary build.

title: Codex logging bug may write TBs to local SSDs
blurb: A GitHub issue reports that OpenAI's Codex CLI continuously writes its SQLite feedback logs to disk at a rate of roughly 640 TB/year, enough to exhaust a consumer SSD's rated write endurance in under a year. The reporter traced about 96% of the writes to verbose TRACE-level logging and mirrored telemetry, including high-frequency inotify and websocket payload entries that filtering could eliminate.

title: PixelSmash – Critical FFmpeg Vulnerability Turns Media Files into Weapons
blurb: JFrog Security Research disclosed PixelSmash (CVE-2026-8461), a critical heap out-of-bounds write in FFmpeg's MagicYUV decoder that escalates from a crash to reliable remote code execution via a single crafted 50KB AVI file. Because nearly every media app links libavcodec, the blast radius spans Jellyfin, Nextcloud, mpv, Kodi, OBS, and more, with researchers demonstrating full RCE against Jellyfin through an automatic library scan.

title: Skip Recompiling 70+ iOS Packages on Every Build
blurb: Expo SDK 56 ships its iOS modules as precompiled XCFrameworks distributed through npm, so apps link prebuilt binaries instead of recompiling React Native core, Expo modules, and native dependencies from source on every clean build. The change works in existing apps with no migration and advances React Native's broader move off Ruby-based CocoaPods, which goes read-only in December 2026, toward Swift Package Manager.

title: How Claude Mythos found a 15-year-old bug in Mozilla Firefox | Brian Grinstead
blurb: Mozilla distinguished engineer Brian Grinstead points an agentic bug-finding pipeline at Firefox's tens of millions of lines of code, shipping a record month of security fixes that included a 15-year-old bug. He argues the harness matters as much as Anthropic's Mythos model, walking through the goal-loop pattern, an LLM judge that ranks files before spending compute, and a verifier subagent that kills false positives.

title: North Korean Hackers Blamed for Mastra NPM Supply Chain Attack
blurb: Microsoft attributed last week's Mastra supply chain attack to North Korean group Sapphire Sleet, which compromised an NPM maintainer account and added a malicious dayjs typosquat to 141 packages totaling roughly 8 million weekly downloads. The obfuscated postinstall dropper ran during npm install on Windows, macOS, and Linux, harvesting system data and targeting 160-plus crypto browser extensions, so any workstation or CI/CD pipeline that installed during the June 17 window is exposed.

title: 잘못된 Claude.md가 AI를 망친다…연구진이 꼽은 설정 파일 6대 문제
blurb: Researchers cataloged six "configuration smells" in Claude.md/Agents.md files, with lint leakage and context bloat the most common.

title: Google Sets Sept. 30 Deadline for Android Developer Verification in Four Countries
blurb: Google will begin enforcing Android developer verification on September 30, 2026, blocking normal installs of apps from unregistered developers on certified devices in Brazil, Indonesia, Singapore, and Thailand, across both Google Play and OEM stores from Samsung, Xiaomi, OPPO, vivo, Honor, and Transsion. The check runs on-device via a new Android Developer Verifier service, hitting independent and open-source channels hardest, with unverified apps installable only through ADB or a deliberately high-friction sideload flow.

title: Using Scroll-Driven Animations for Opposing Scroll Directions
blurb: This post shows how to build columns of items scrolling in opposite directions using CSS scroll-driven animations, with no JavaScript required. The author walks through the markup, a CSS mask-based fade at the column edges, and per-column animation-range timing to drive the opposing motion, noting the demo respects reduced-motion settings and currently works in Chrome and Safari.

title: Memory Safe Inline Assembly
blurb: Fil-C, the memory-safe C dialect, now supports GCC/clang inline assembly syntax in a pre-release build, preserving the exact assembly template you write while guaranteeing memory safety. The post details how mistakes that would silently miscompile in standard C, like omitting a `+` constraint or a `cc` clobber, instead degrade to a panic or an illegal instruction trap at worst.

title: Show HN: Recall – Local project memory for Claude Code
blurb: Recall is a local memory plugin for Claude Code that logs each session to an append-only `history.md` and condenses it into a compact `context.md` summary using a classical Python summarizer rather than an LLM call. It solves the cold-start problem without spending model tokens or sending transcripts to any external API, so resuming a project loads from a ~1-2K token digest instead of re-explaining everything each session.

title: Good results fine tuning a local LLM like Qwen 3:0.6B to categorize questions
blurb: The author fine-tuned Qwen 3:0.6B with Unsloth on ~850 household questions to classify queries into metadata categories like pool, car, and HVAC, using the labels to narrow the vector search space before a RAG lookup. The post benchmarks the tuned 600M-parameter classifier against a prompt-only baseline across ~130 integration tests, walking through the 70/15/15 train/eval/test split and the accuracy gains from fine-tuning.

title: PP-OCRv6 on Hugging Face: 50-Language OCR from 1.5M to 34.5M Parameters
blurb: PaddleOCR released PP-OCRv6, a universal OCR model family scaling from 1.5M to 34.5M parameters across tiny, small, and medium tiers, with the small and medium tiers covering 50 languages including Chinese, English, Japanese, and 46 Latin-script languages. The medium tier hits 86.2% detection Hmean and 83.2% recognition accuracy on PaddleOCR's in-house benchmarks, improving on PP-OCRv5_server by 4.6 and 5.1 points respectively, with PaddlePaddle, Transformers, and ONNX Runtime deployment backends.

title: There is minimal downside to switching to open models
blurb: Drawing an analogy to the years when running Linux carried real professional risk, the author argues that open-weight LLMs have closed enough of the gap that switching away from Claude and GPT now costs little, a shift prompted by Claude's ID verification rollout. The remaining trade-offs are concrete: open models still trail proprietary ones on leaderboards, and self-hosting to preserve privacy is some combination of expensive, complicated, and slow.

title: The only scalable delete in Postgres is DROP TABLE
blurb: The author argues that large DELETEs in Postgres are "work added, not work done," since MVCC leaves dead tuples behind that don't free disk space, force replication overhead, and burden other writers until autovacuum catches up. The recommendation is to design schemas so bulk cleanup can be expressed as a DROP TABLE or TRUNCATE, which reclaim space immediately instead of leaving the heap and indexes to garbage-collect later.

title: 23 ClawHub plugins squatting official scopes expose AI registry security gaps
blurb: Researchers found 23 code-executing ClawHub plugins squatting the official @openclaw and @clawhub scopes while owned by unrelated accounts.
